text:
“I don’t have any concern about it,” he said in an interview with Reuters earlier this month. “They’ll drop very rapidly when this is over, and if they rise, they rise, but this is far more important than having gasoline prices go up a little bit.”
The world has changed in some ways that will soften the blow. Improvements in energy efficiency and large oil stockpiles around the world serve as a buffer, and alternative energy sources mean oil is less important to the global economy than it was in the 1970s.
The United States does not rely on the Middle East for oil, but other economies, notably in Asia, do and are now competitors for oil on the global market. So even if the closing of the Strait of Hormuz won’t lead to lines for gasoline in the United States, the longer the conflict lasts, the more difficult the ramifications are to predict.
title: Britain warns its citizens in Dubai they could be jailed for sharing photos of damage from airstrikes.

citability.title: Britain warns its citizens in Dubai they could be jailed for sharing photos of damage from airstrikes.
